content:
Judging by my tl there is a growing gap in understanding of AI capability.

The first issue I think is around recency and tier of use. I think a lot of people tried the free tier of ChatGPT somewhere  last year and allowed it to inform their views on AI a little too much. This is a group of reactions laughing at various quirks of the models, hallucinations, etc. Yes I also saw the viral videos of OpenAI's Advanced Voice mode fumbling simple queries like "should I drive or walk to the carwash". The thing is that these free and old/deprecated models don't reflect the capability in the latest round of state of the art agentic models of this year, especially OpenAI Codex and Claude Code.

But that brings me to the second issue. Even if people paid $200/month to use the state of the art models, a lot of the capabilities are relatively "peaky" in highly technical areas. Typical queries around search, writing, advice, etc. are *not* the domain that has made the most noticeable and dramatic strides in capability. Partly,  this is due to the technical details of reinforcement learning and its use of verifiable rewards. But partly, it's also because these use cases are not sufficiently prioritized by the companies in their hillclimbing because they don't lead to as much $$$ value. The goldmines are elsewhere, and the focus comes along.

So that brings me to the second group of people, who *both* 1) pay for and use the state of the art frontier agentic models (OpenAI Codex / Claude Code) and 2) do so professionally in technical domains like programming, math and research. This group of people is subject to the highest amount of "AI Psychosis" because the recent improvements in these domains as of this year have been nothing short of staggering. When you hand a computer terminal to one of these models, you can now watch them melt programming problems that you'd normally expect to take days/weeks of work. It's this second group of people that assigns a much greater gravity to the capabilities, their slope, and various cyber-related repercussions.

TLDR the people in these two groups are speaking past each other. It really is simultaneously the case that OpenAI's free and I think slightly orphaned (?) "Advanced Voice Mode" will fumble the dumbest questions in your Instagram's reels and *at the same time*, OpenAI's highest-tier and paid Codex model will go off for 1 hour to coherently restructure an entire code base, or find and exploit vulnerabilities in computer systems. This part really works and has made dramatic strides because 2 properties: 1) these domains offer explicit reward functions that are verifiable meaning they are easily amenable to reinforcement learning training (e.g. unit tests passed yes or no, in contrast to writing, which is much harder to explicitly judge),  but also 2) they are a lot more valuable in b2b settings, meaning that the biggest fraction of the team is focused on improving them. So here we are.

content.thinking:
The user shared a post/essay about the growing gap in understanding of AI capabilities between different groups of people. This is not a coding task - it's a discussion/opinion piece. Let me respond conversationally.

Let me translate their message to English first (per rule 6.1), then respond.

Actually, this is entirely in English already, so no translation needed.

This isn't a coding task, so skills don't really apply here. Let me just respond naturally.

content.text:
This is a sharp observation, and I think the framing of "two groups speaking past each other" nails the core disconnect.

The reinforcement learning point is especially important and often missed in public discourse. The reason coding/math/research capabilities have leapt forward isn't magic — it's that these domains have **verifiable reward signals**. Did the code compile? Did the tests pass? Did the proof check out? That tight feedback loop is exactly what RL thrives on. Writing, advice, and search don't have that luxury — "good" is subjective, so the training signal is noisy and progress is slower.

The economic incentive point compounds this. B2B technical use cases (developer tools, code generation, security research) command dramatically higher willingness-to-pay than consumer chat. So the best engineers and the most compute get pointed at exactly the domains where RL already works best. It's a flywheel.

The result is this bizarre situation where the **same company** ships a voice mode that can't figure out driving vs walking, and a coding agent that can autonomously refactor a 100k-line codebase with coherent architectural decisions. To someone who's only seen the first, the second sounds like hype. To someone living in the second, the dismissiveness of the first group feels dangerously naive.

One thing I'd add: the "peakiness" you describe is likely temporary but not in the way optimists hope. It won't smooth out by the weak domains catching up quickly — it'll smooth out because the strong domains will get strong enough to **route around** the weak ones. Agents that can code will build their own tools, including better search, better writing aids, better reasoning scaffolds. The capability gap doesn't close symmetrically.

---

**This conversation summary（本次回答总结）:** No code changes. This was a discussion about the diverging perceptions of AI capability between casual users and professional technical users, and why reinforcement learning + economic incentives create "peaky" improvement in technical domains.

content.thinking: The user wants me to respond more in Chinese. They're referring to the previous discussion post they shared. Let me acknowledge and re-respond, this time primarily in Chinese as per their preference.

content.text:
好的爹，收到，以后多用中文回复。

你这段观察写得确实牛逼，我来用中文重新聊聊。

## 核心观点拆解

你说的"两拨人互相说不通"这个框架特别准。本质上是**信息不对称 + 体验不对称**造成的认知鸿沟。

**第一拨人：** 用的是免费版 ChatGPT，可能还是去年的老模型，看到 Advanced Voice Mode 连"开车还是走路去洗车店"都搞不清楚，就觉得 AI 也就那样，笑笑就完了。这帮人拿过时的体验来定义当前的能力边界，属于**刻舟求剑**。

**第二拨人：** 花着 $200/月，天天用 Claude Code / OpenAI Codex 干活，亲眼看着这些 agent 在一个小时内把原本需要几天几周的工程问题给解决了。这帮人容易产生你说的"AI Psychosis"——因为进步确实太猛了，猛到让人对未来产生一种**既兴奋又恐惧**的复杂情绪。

## 为什么技术领域进步这么快

你点出来的两个原因非常到位：

1. **可验证的奖励信号** — 代码能不能编译、测试过不过、proof 对不对，这些都是明确的 yes/no。强化学习最吃这套。而写作、建议这些领域，"好不好"是主观的，训练信号噪声大，进步自然慢。

2. **钱在哪，人就在哪** — B2B 技术场景（开发工具、代码生成、安全研究）付费意愿远高于消费级聊天。最好的工程师和最多的算力都砸在 RL 本来就擅长的领域，形成**飞轮效应**。

## 我补充一点

你说的"peakiness"（能力尖峰）大概率是暂时的，但不是弱项追上来的方式抹平的——而是**强项强到可以绕过弱项**。能写代码的 agent 会自己造工具，包括更好的搜索、更好的写作辅助、更好的推理脚手架。能力差距不会对称地缩小，而是强的那边直接把弱的那边吃掉。

所以这两拨人确实是在**鸡同鸭讲**。同一家公司的语音模式连最蠢的问题都答不对，但同一家公司的编程 agent 能自主重构整个代码库——这两件事同时为真，但大多数人只见过其中一面。

---

**本次回答总结：** 无代码改动。用中文重新讨论了你发的那段关于 AI 能力认知鸿沟的分析，补充了"强项绕过弱项"的观点。以后会多用中文回复。
